elements.sent-2411-001: We establish interior regularity results for first-order, stationary, local mean-field game (MFG) systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Specifically, we study solutions of the coupled system consisting of a Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman equation $H(x, Du, m) = 0$ and a transport equation $-\div(m D_pH(x, Du, m)) = 0$ in a domain $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R ^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-003: Under suitable structural assumptions on the Hamiltonian $H$, without requiring monotonicity of the system, convexity of the Hamiltonian, separability in variables, or smoothness beyond basic continuity in $(p,m)$, we introduce a notion of weak solutions that allows the application of techniques from elliptic regularity theory.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our main contribution is to prove that the value function $u$ is locally Hölder continuous in $\Omega$.
elements.sent-2411-005: The proof leverages the connection between first-order MFG systems and quasilinear equations in divergence form, adapting classical techniques to handle the specific structure of MFG systems.
elements.sent-2411-006: Mean-field games (MFG) provide a mathematical framework for modeling large populations of rational agents, each optimizing their individual objectives while being influenced by the collective behavior of the population.
elements.sent-2411-007: These systems have found widespread applications across multiple disciplines.
elements.sent-2411-008: In economics, they model price formation \cite{MR4215224, FT20, FTT20 , market dynamics \cite{cardaliaguetMeanFieldGame2018 , and wealth distribution \cite{GLasryLionsMoll .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Engineering applications include the analysis of traffic flow \cite{GomesMS19 , pedestrian dynamics \cite{Bagagiolo2022 , and smart grid coordination \cite{KizilkaleSM19, Kizilkale2014829 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: In social sciences, MFGs have been instrumental in understanding opinion dynamics \cite{Bauso20143475, Bolouki2014556, Stella20132519 , segregation patterns \cite{AcCiMa , and knowledge diffusion \cite{lucasmoll .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: A key advantage of MFG theory is its ability to reduce the complexity of large-scale multi-agent systems to tractable systems of partial differential equations (PDEs), enabling both qualitative analysis and numerical simulation.
elements.sent-2411-012: This mathematical structure has sparked extensive research, leading to various theoretical developments and model extensions.
elements.sent-2411-013: This article investigates the interior regularity of solutions for first-order, stationary, local mean-field game systems.
elements.sent-2411-014: The study of regularity properties in MFG systems extends beyond theoretical interest, playing a fundamental role in these systems' analytical and computational aspects.
elements.sent-2411-015: From a theoretical perspective, regularity results provide essential tools for establishing the existence and uniqueness of solutions and offer insights into solution stability.
elements.sent-2411-016: From a numerical perspective, regularity properties ensure the convergence and accuracy of discretization schemes, preventing spurious behaviors that could arise in numerical approximations.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our primary contribution is the proof of Hölder continuity for solutions of the following MFG model (Theorem \ref{thm:holder ).}
elements.sent-2411-018: We assume that for almost all $x\in\Omega$, the mappings $(p,m) \mapsto H(x, p, m)$ and $(p,m) \mapsto D_pH(x, p, m)$ are continuous.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our MFG system consists of two coupled equations: \begin{empheq [left=\empheqlbrace]{alignat=2 H(x, Du, m) = 0 \text{in}\enskip \Omega, -\div(m D_pH(x, Du, m)) = 0 \qquad \text{in}\enskip \Omega.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Section~\ref{sec:prelim establishes the mathematical framework for our analysis, introducing fundamental definitions, notation, and key assumptions on the Hamiltonian $H$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: In Section~\ref{subsec:assume , we present a set of structural conditions on $H$ (Assumption~\ref{assume ) that enable us to develop a notion of weak solutions (Definition~\ref{def:solution ) and facilitate the proof of our main regularity results.}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: A distinguishing feature of our framework is the remarkable mildness of these assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-023: We do not require $H(x,p,m)$ to be separable; that is, $H$ of the form $H(x,p,m)=H_0(x,p)-g(m)$.
elements.sent-2411-024: In addition, we do not have any monotonicity constraints, nor do we impose smoothness conditions beyond basic continuity in $(p,m)$.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our analysis permits $H$ to be discontinuous in the spatial variable $x$ and requires neither differentiability in $m$ nor twice-differentiability in $p$.
elements.sent-2411-026: This level of generality significantly extends beyond standard examples like \begin{equation}\label{standardH} H(x,p,m) = \frac{|p|^{\alpha}}{m^\tau} - m^{\beta},\end{equation} where $\alpha$, $\tau$, and $\beta$ are fixed real numbers satisfying \eqref{par1 , and encompasses a broad class of Hamiltonians that may exhibit anisotropic behavior, non-monotonicity, and non-smoothness.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Such flexibility allows our results to apply to problems where standard regularity assumptions may be too restrictive while maintaining sufficient structure to establish meaningful regularity results for the solutions.
elements.sent-2411-028: In particular, \eqref{par1 does not imply Lions' monotonicity condition that he derived in his lecture series \cite{LCDF , which reduces to $\tau\alpha^\prime \leq 4$ for the particular example in \eqref{standardH .}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: The mathematical theory of mean-field games emerged from two independent lines of research.
elements.sent-2411-030: The term was introduced by Lasry and Lions in their seminal papers \cite{ll1,ll2,lasryMeanFieldGames2007a , which laid the mathematical foundations of the field.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Concurrently, Caines, Huang, and Malhamé \cite{HuangCainesMalhame06,Caines2 developed similar concepts from an engineering perspective, focusing on control-theoretic applications.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Comprehensive presentations of the theory can be found in the course materials \cite{LCDF , lecture notes \cite{cardaliaguet2018short , and the survey \cite{Caines2017 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The framework has since been extended to encompass more complex scenarios, including systems with major and minor players \cite{Caines2, MA2020108774 , multi-population dynamics \cite{cirant , and finite-state problems \cite{GMS, GMS2 , for example.}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: MFG System~\ref{main describes the collective behavior of a large population of rational agents who act independently and competitively.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Each agent aims to minimize their individual cost, which depends on their interactions with both the population distribution and the environment, as they navigate toward potential exits from the domain.
elements.sent-2411-036: This optimization occurs in a non-cooperative setting, where agents make decisions based on their own objectives rather than coordinating with others.
elements.sent-2411-037: Given a stationary probability distribution $m:\Omega\to \Rr^+_0$, consider a single agent who knows this distribution and seeks to minimize their total cost.
elements.sent-2411-038: For an initial position $x\in\Omega$, this cost is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:CostI} \mathcal{I}[x,\mathbf{v}]:= \int_{0}^{T_E} L( \mathbf{x}(t),\mathbf{v}(t),m(\mathbf{x}(t))) dt +\psi(\bx(T_E)) 1_{T_E<+\infty}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-039: The value function $u(x)$ represents the minimal cost achievable from position $x$: \begin{equation}u(x) = \inf_{\mathbf{v}(\cdot)} \mathcal{I}[x,\mathbf{v}].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-040: The exit time $T_E$ may be infinite, indicating that the agent might never exit the domain.
elements.sent-2411-041: When $T_E$ is finite, the exit cost $\psi:\partial \Omega\to \Rr$ determines the Dirichlet boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-042: Since our analysis focuses on interior regularity, the specific choice of boundary conditions (whether Dirichlet or state constraints) is not essential.
elements.sent-2411-043: The instantaneous cost of an agent's actions is encoded in the function $L$, called the Lagrangian or running cost.
elements.sent-2411-044: This function captures the direct costs of the agent's actions and the indirect costs arising from interactions with other agents through the density $m$.
elements.sent-2411-045: The Lagrangian is mathematically related to the Hamiltonian $H$ through the Legendre transform: \begin{equation}\label{lt} H(x,p,m):= \sup_{v} (-p\cdot v -L(x,v,m)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-046: This transformation is crucial in converting the agent's optimization problem into the Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman equation in our MFG system.
elements.sent-2411-047: Moreover, a Lagrangian can be recovered via the same transformation: \begin{equation}L(x,v,m):= \sup_{p} (-v\cdot p -H(x,p,m)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-048: Applying this to the model case \eqref{standardH , we obtain: \begin{equation}L(x,v,m) = m^{\frac{\tau}{\alpha-1}}\frac{|v|^{\alpha'}}{\alpha'} + m^{\beta}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Here, the effect of the exponents in \eqref{standardH becomes more evident.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Specifically, the exponent $\tau$ quantifies velocity-dependent congestion effects, representing the cost of moving at speed $v$ in density $m(x)$, while the exponent $\beta$ measures position-dependent congestion effects, indicating the cost of remaining at position $x$ in density $m(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-051: As such, \eqref{par1 implies that the position-dependent congestion effect is dominant.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Under the assumption of optimal behavior, each agent minimizes their individual cost as determined by the value function $u(x)$, which represents the minimal cost-to-go from position~$x$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Optimal control theory establishes that $u$ satisfies the Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman (HJB) equation \eqref{eq:hjb in the viscosity sense.}
elements.sent-2411-054: When the value function is sufficiently regular, the optimal control strategy takes the feedback form \begin{equation}\bv(t)= -D_pH(\bx(t), Du(\bx(t)), m(\bx(t))),\end{equation} where $D_pH$ represents the gradient of $H$ with respect to the momentum variable.
elements.sent-2411-055: For rational agents with a time-independent distribution $m$, the collective behavior of the population is described by the transport equation \eqref{eq:fp , which determines the agent density.}
elements.sent-2411-056: The coupling between these equations, the HJB equation describing individual optimization and the transport equation determining collective dynamics, constitutes our MFG system.
elements.sent-2411-057: The regularity theory for MFG systems takes different forms depending on whether the equations are elliptic, parabolic, or first-order.
elements.sent-2411-058: In elliptic and parabolic MFG systems, each equation can be analyzed separately using classical regularity theory.
elements.sent-2411-059: In contrast, first-order MFG systems behave as a single elliptic equation in the value function $u$.
elements.sent-2411-060: This structural property directly links the regularity theory of first-order MFGs and that of elliptic equations, suggesting that techniques from elliptic regularity theory should be effective in analyzing these systems.
elements.sent-2411-061: For separated MFGs, this connection is particularly transparent through their variational formulation, see, e.g., \cite{GS .}
elements.sent-2411-062: Functionals of the form \begin{equation}\label{eq:var-functional} \int_\Omega G(H(x,Du)) \, dx,\end{equation} where $G$ is strictly convex and increasing, yield Euler-Lagrange equations that can be written as MFG systems: \begin{equation}\label{eq:mfg-system} \begin{cases} H(x,Du) = (G')^{-1}(m) \text{in } \Omega, [1ex] -\dive(m D_pH(x, Du)) = 0 \text{in } \Omega. \end{cases}\end{equation} A canonical example is the $\gamma$-Laplace equation, which corresponds to choosing $H(x,p) = \frac{2 {\gamma |p|^{\gamma/2}$ and $G(z) = \frac{1 {2 z^2$: \begin{equation}\label{eq:p-laplace-mfg} \begin{cases} \dfrac{2}{\gamma} |D u|^{\gamma/2} = m \text{in } \Omega, [1ex] -\dive(m |D u|^{\gamma/2-2} D u) = 0 \text{in } \Omega. \end{cases}\end{equation} Variational methods have proven particularly fruitful in developing the theory of first-order MFGs.}}}}
